behaviors.description: Invalid signature (mismatch with body) is rejected with 401; body not forwarded to queue
behaviors.justification: Real failures live in HTTP request body capture (raw bytes, encoding issues), signature comparison logic, and queue integration. Unit tests of isolated crypto functions miss raw-body encoding bugs and whether rejection prevents forwarding. Must test with real HTTP request object (preserving raw body for signature calculation) wired to both signature validation and queue integration.
behaviors.test_description: Send HTTP POST with intentionally mismatched X-Signature-256 header (e.g., header contains SHA256 of body+'tampered'). Verify 401 returned and queue.enqueue() never called. Then send same body with correct signature, verify 200 and event in queue.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration catches the critical intersection: raw body handling + signature validation + queue prevention.

behaviors.description: Event is written to persistent queue before HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned
behaviors.justification: This is a race condition between two I/O operations: queue.write() and response.send(). Can't test at Unit (no I/O). Must wire handler, queue client, and HTTP response together. The critical failure is 200 being sent before queue confirms write. A test-double queue that tracks completion order and a real HTTP test server capture this. Note: If the persistent queue is an external broker (Kafka/RabbitMQ with network latency), System testing may expose real failures that Integration with a local queue misses.
behaviors.test_description: Instrument queue.write() and response.send() with timing hooks. Send a request that triggers queue persistence. Capture timestamps and verify queue.write() callback completes (returns promise/callback) before response headers are flushed to the client.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration with timing instrumentation catches order bugs; System-level testing with actual message broker would be more robust for truly persistent external queues.

behaviors.description: Handler returns HTTP 200 response without waiting for downstream processing to complete
behaviors.justification: The failure is handler code that synchronously awaits downstream processing (blocking the response). Unit test of handler logic alone, if not carefully constructed with a non-completing mock, can miss synchronous calls or improper async handling. Real failure boundary is the handler wired with a slow/blocking downstream: need to observe that response is sent while downstream is still pending. Mocking downstream as instant would hide the async requirement.
behaviors.test_description: Create a downstream processor mock that accepts the async call but never resolves (e.g., Promise that never settles). Send request, measure HTTP response latency (expect <100ms). Verify response arrives before downstream completes. Check downstream was invoked (via spy) but didn't block response.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration with a never-resolving downstream mock reliably catches synchronous blocking or improper async/await patterns.

behaviors.description: HTTP response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when earlier events are undergoing slow downstream processing and requests are concurrent
behaviors.justification: This is fundamentally OS-level concurrency behavior. Real failures hide in lock contention, thread starvation, or blocking in shared code paths. An in-process mock of slow processing won't expose these—the mock runs in the same thread/event loop. Wall-clock timing under real concurrent OS load is the only way to detect hidden synchronous code or resource exhaustion. Unit and Integration tests with local mocks can pass even if the real code blocks under load.
behaviors.test_description: Spawn 10+ concurrent HTTP requests via test server or load tool. Send request #1 with downstream that sleeps 2+ seconds. Immediately send requests #2-10 with fast downstream. Measure actual response times for all requests. Verify p99 latency is <3 seconds, even while request #1 is slow.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: System-level concurrent load is the only reliable way to expose timing bugs and contention issues that mocks hide.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time (deduplication)
behaviors.justification: The failure is deduplication logic (check seen IDs, skip if seen). Unit test of dedup logic alone could miss the persistent ID store interaction. Real failure comes from: (a) not actually storing/checking against the ID store, (b) race condition where two identical IDs arrive concurrently and both pass the check before either is stored. Integration with a real ID store (or in-process test double) and concurrent request simulation catches both.
behaviors.test_description: Send first request with event ID 'ev_abc_123', verify it's enqueued. Send a second request with the same ID. Verify the second request is rejected or silently dropped (ID not re-enqueued). Query queue and confirm only one copy of 'ev_abc_123' exists. Also test concurrent race: send two requests with the same ID in parallel; verify only one reaches the queue.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration with a persistent ID store and concurrent request execution catches both logic and race-condition failures.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 Service Unavailable with Retry-After header
behaviors.justification: Handler logic: 'if queue reports full, return 503 with Retry-After.' Could be tested at Unit with a mocked queue that reports isFull()=true. But real failure might hide in: (a) handler doesn't check isFull(), (b) handler catches the exception but returns wrong status/headers, (c) queue reports full via exception, not a method. Integration test wiring handler with a queue that actually reports full (via mock or real library) catches all these.
behaviors.test_description: Configure queue mock to return true for queue.isFull(). Send a request. Verify response status is 503. Verify Retry-After header is present and contains a valid duration (e.g., 60 seconds).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration catches integration logic bugs; Unit could work but is riskier if the real queue's API differs from the mock.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it
behaviors.justification: This tests the queue itself, not the handler. Real failure: queue accepts more than capacity. Can't test with a mocked queue that enforces capacity (that tests the mock, not the real behavior). Need a real queue library (in-process) or a realistic test double that actually implements capacity logic. If the persistent queue is an external message broker, System testing (against real Kafka/RabbitMQ) is required.
behaviors.test_description: Instantiate queue with capacity=5. Enqueue 5 events successfully (verify all 5 accepted). Attempt to enqueue a 6th event. Verify it's rejected (throws error or returns error status). Confirm queue still contains exactly 5 events (no overflow, no silent drops).
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration assumes in-process queue library with real capacity logic. If queue is external (message broker), System testing required.
